<skill>
<name>retro</name>
<path>/Users/alien/Projects/skills/retro/SKILL.md</path>
---
name: retro
description: End-of-session retrospective. Extracts failure modes, environment struggles, and tooling proposals. Use after completing a work session, when asked to reflect on what happened, or when the user says "retro" or "retrospective".
user-invocable: true
argument-hint: '[project or focus area]'
effort: medium
---

# Session Retrospective

Review this session systematically. The goal is error
So is there things left to download?
agent_messages: Using `researcher` plus the required `modal` and `genomics-pipeline` skills. I’m starting by inventorying what this repo already uses locally and on Modal/SSD, then I’ll compare that against current datasets, ontologies, algorithms, and models that look additive rather than redundant.

This is a standard-to-deep research pass. I’m splitting it into parallel tracks: local inventory of what the pipeline already uses, external candidates for datasets/ontologies, and external candidates for algorithms/models, then I’ll merge them into a gap-driven memo rather than a tool dump.

I’ve started three parallel tracks. While they run, I’m grounding the answer in the repo itself: docs, config, stage metadata, and the current local/volume layout so recommendations map to what already exists instead of repeating it.

The repo inventory is bigger than the top-level docs suggest. The local status script confirms a large set of downstream analyses already exist, and it also exposed a concrete issue: `scripts/genomics_status.py --volume` is currently failing on April 2, 2026 because it decorates a non-global functio

The recent docs matter more than older gap memos here. A lot of March 22 “missing” items were already integrated or superseded by April 1 decisions, so I’m reading the newest acquisition/placement memos to avoid re-proposing things you already have.

I’ve got the internal baseline now: ontology normalization shipped locally, Open Targets bulk is only partial, eQTL Catalogue helpers exist but the mirror is incomplete, and SpliceVault/OLIDA are the remaining substantive substrate gaps. I’m cross-checking those against primary external sources so t

The repo already contains stage stubs for PARM and SuPreMo, plus MPRA and ClinMAVE acquisition scripts. I’m validating whether those are “missing methods” or just “missing substrate/benchmark completion,” because those are different recommendations.

The stale-doc problem is now part of the task. I’m folding it into the memo as a separate section: not just what to add, but how to stop March recommendations from surviving into April after the code and SSD state have already changed.

The first external pass converges with the repo: no urgent ontology gap remains. The real missing substrate is cross-study QTLs, reproducible trait-to-gene ranking, oligogenic expansion beyond DIDA, and a few benchmark-oriented truth layers rather than more taxonomy.

I’m waiting on the local-inventory and algorithms/model passes, but the shape is already clear enough to avoid a bad answer: stop treating ontologies as the main gap, and stop adding frontier scorers until the benchmark substrate catches up.

agent_messages: Using `researcher` plus the required `modal` and `genomics-pipeline` skills. I’m starting by inventorying what this repo already uses locally and on Modal/SSD, then I’ll compare that against current datasets, ontologies, algorithms, and models that look additive rather than redundant.

This is a standard-to-deep research pass. I’m splitting it into parallel tracks: local inventory of what the pipeline already uses, external candidates for datasets/ontologies, and external candidates for algorithms/models, then I’ll merge them into a gap-driven memo rather than a tool dump.

I’ve started three parallel tracks. While they run, I’m grounding the answer in the repo itself: docs, config, stage metadata, and the current local/volume layout so recommendations map to what already exists instead of repeating it.

The repo inventory is bigger than the top-level docs suggest. The local status script confirms a large set of downstream analyses already exist, and it also exposed a concrete issue: `scripts/genomics_status.py --volume` is currently failing on April 2, 2026 because it decorates a non-global functio

The recent docs matter more than older gap memos here. A lot of March 22 “missing” items were already integrated or superseded by April 1 decisions, so I’m reading the newest acquisition/placement memos to avoid re-proposing things you already have.

I’ve got the internal baseline now: ontology normalization shipped locally, Open Targets bulk is only partial, eQTL Catalogue helpers exist but the mirror is incomplete, and SpliceVault/OLIDA are the remaining substantive substrate gaps. I’m cross-checking those against primary external sources so t

The repo already contains stage stubs for PARM and SuPreMo, plus MPRA and ClinMAVE acquisition scripts. I’m validating whether those are “missing methods” or just “missing substrate/benchmark completion,” because those are different recommendations.

The stale-doc problem is now part of the task. I’m folding it into the memo as a separate section: not just what to add, but how to stop March recommendations from surviving into April after the code and SSD state have already changed.

The first external pass converges with the repo: no urgent ontology gap remains. The real missing substrate is cross-study QTLs, reproducible trait-to-gene ranking, oligogenic expansion beyond DIDA, and a few benchmark-oriented truth layers rather than more taxonomy.

I’m waiting on the local-inventory and algorithms/model passes, but the shape is already clear enough to avoid a bad answer: stop treating ontologies as the main gap, and stop adding frontier scorers until the benchmark substrate catches up.

agent_messages: Using `modal`, `genomics-pipeline`, and `researcher` for this turn. I’m checking the current pipeline state and recent commits first, then I’ll map concrete integration opportunities against what the repo already has so the recommendations aren’t redundant.

Tier: Standard. I have the research and evidence guardrails loaded; next I’m grounding this in the repo’s actual current state so the integration list reflects what landed in the last few commits rather than generic genomics wishlists.

The local state is broad already: most of the obvious single-source add-ons are present, including REMM, V2P, MotifDiff, allele age, selection pressure, digenic, TAD disruption, peptidome, mechanistic cards, and several phenotype panels. I’m drilling into the last research commits and current docs n

Recent commits already landed frontier hooks like `alphagenome`, `esm_lfb`, `flashzoi`, `parm`, `supremo_sv`, and acquisition plumbing. That changes the answer materially: the remaining gaps are less “another predictor” and more “better reference priors, ontologies, and validation surfaces.”

I’m checking the older gap memos against the new April state, because several March “missing” items are now stale. The question isn’t “what exists in the world,” it’s “what’s still missing after the last two research and pipeline sweeps.”

Some “recommendations” from the April memo are already half-landed in code: there are fetch/lookup helpers for Open Targets bulk, eQTL Catalogue, and SpliceVault. I’m checking whether those are just scaffolding or already have data and callers, because that changes whether they belong in “new integr

Local picture is clearer now: ontology normalization is shipped; GTEx-backed tissue convergence is live; Open Targets/eQTL Catalogue/SpliceVault have helper scripts but no mirrored datasets or first-class stage surfaces yet. I’m doing a quick external confirmation pass on the top remaining candidate

I have enough signal on the big missing layers. I’m doing one last pass for secondary additions that are not already in code, because several March suggestions like DGIdb and GeneBayes are now stale and shouldn’t clutter the recommendation list.

## Integration Gaps — Research Memo

**Question:** After the newest state and git log, what is still worth integrating?  
**Tier:** Standard | **Date:** 2026-04-01

**Ground truth:** `HEAD` is `1910ba2` on `main`. The important April 1 commits were `c14fde7` and `56f40e4`: they added ontology/tissue

I’m treating this as a claim audit, not a rewrite. I’ll re-check the recommendations against three things: current repo state, primary docs/papers, and an independent web-grounded synthesis so anything weak gets downgraded.

The next pass is narrowing the local inventory. I’m not treating this as a blank-slate scan; I’m reading the existing research memos and pipeline surfaces first so the recommendations are incremental rather than recycled.

I’ve confirmed the repo already has multiple overlapping scans, including one from yesterday. Now I’m checking the actual code and local database surfaces, because “researched” and “implemented” are different states in this repo.

The implemented surface is broader than the older memos suggest. Several items previously framed as “future work” already exist as stages or scripts, including `esm_lfb`, `gtex_eqtl`, `dida_digenic`, `mavedb`, `encode_ccre`, `abc_enhancer`, `predicted_expression`, and dbNSFP-backed VEP.
I’ve separated three states now: already implemented, already researched but not built, and still genuinely new. The next search round is only for the third bucket.

I’ve pinned the current implemented baseline. Next I’m verifying which outside resources are actually reachable and bulk-usable, because a lot of genomics “datasets” turn out to be portals, APIs, or access-restricted papers rather than something we can wire into a stage.

The remaining ambiguity is execution state: some stages exist in code, but the repo has several “implemented but not yet run/downloaded” surfaces. I’m checking the actual mounted volume now so the memo can distinguish code presence from data presence.

This clarified a lot. `predictdb` is already downloaded locally despite an older memo saying otherwise, but `abc_model`, `encode_ccre`, `uniref50`, and `dbnsfp` are still absent on the mounted database volume. `gtex_eqtl` exists as a results directory, but I still need to check whether that reflects

The search is converging. The strongest new bucket now looks like cross-study QTL resources, trait-to-gene datasets, and ontology cleanup layers that would make the existing tissue/cell-type annotations interoperable instead of ad hoc.

I have enough to write the memo. It will explicitly separate “already implemented,” “already researched but not active,” and “genuinely new additions,” because that distinction is the main failure mode here.

agent_messages: Using `modal`, `genomics-pipeline`, `researcher`, and `dispatch-research` in that order. First I’m checking what the pipeline already does locally, then I’ll look outward for genuinely newer WGS analyses worth adding instead of rebranding existing stages.

I’ve loaded the research and domain guardrails. Next I’m inventorying what the repo already supports so the “new” recommendations are actually net-new, then I’ll cross-check that against recent methods and tools.

The local inventory is already useful: the repo has broad coverage, including AlphaGenome, Evo2, pangenome CNV genotyping, MEI calling, branchpoint scans, predicted expression, and several exploratory somatic/noncoding layers. The real question is no longer “what analyses exist,” it’s “which analyse

This is a `Deep` research pass. I’m splitting the search into three axes: short-read analyses we can run now, newer methods that meaningfully extend current WGS interpretation, and high-value analyses that need a new assay so we don’t confuse “new” with “actually possible from the current 30 GB.”

Semantic Scholar is 403’ing, which matches the skill’s fallback note. I’m switching the paper search to OpenAlex and direct preprint/publisher search so this stays grounded in actual sources instead of stale memory.

I’m dispatching three bounded codebase audits in parallel: somatic/mosaic, tissue/phenotype-conditioned interpretation, and next-gen PRS. The point is to separate “nice paper” from “actually a gap in this repo.”

The pattern is getting clearer. The most interesting additions are not another generic score layer; they’re analyses that change the decision surface: low-VAF somatic calling, tissue/phenotype-conditioned interpretation that is less proxy-like, and WGS-native PRS that uses regulatory context instead
The code audit is still running. I’m not blocked on it, so I’m using the time to tighten the external evidence and then I’ll pull the repo-specific conclusions back in before writing the memo.

The PRS audit confirmed the likely gap: the repo already does score-file PRS and exploratory pathway decomposition, but not a true WGS-native common+rare or regulatory-feature PRS layer. I’m waiting briefly on the somatic and tissue-conditioned audits, then I’ll rank the best net-new analyses by nov

The somatic audit landed and it changes the framing slightly: DeepSomatic is already in the repo, so the opportunity is not another caller but a more defensible somatic layer on top of weak saliva 30x evidence. I’m waiting one last beat on the tissue/phenotype audit because that’s the other likely f

agent_messages: Using `researcher` first and `dispatch-research` second. I’m loading the relevant skill instructions and reading the existing expansion plan so I can avoid repeating work and target genuinely new analysis ideas.

The existing April 2 plan already claims the obvious next tranche: blood groups, `LPA`, predicted expression, and cautious mCA. I’m narrowing to orthogonal surfaces the repo has not already promoted, then I’ll dispatch a few bounded scouts so the ideas are not all coming from one search axis.

The repo already has a lot of March scanning. I’m now doing a gap pass against those memos and the codebase so the next memo only includes surfaces that are both novel to this project and plausible from Nebula 30x saliva WGS.

A useful pattern is emerging: the repo already has several latent analyses in code that were never made first-class, and a second bucket of genuinely new analyses from recent literature. I’m separating those, because “new idea” and “new implementation work” are not the same thing.

`dispatch-research` normally means parallel scouting, but the host is already at `9` active `claude|codex` processes. I’m keeping delegation minimal and using one bounded scout rather than fanning out and making the session slower or noisier.

I’ve got four candidate buckets worth validating against primary sources: complex hard loci, haplotype/history analyses, mtDNA/STR/MEI process layers, and immunogenomic/self-peptide ideas. I’m pulling recent papers on the strongest ones now so the next memo ranks things by evidence instead of novelt

I’ve confirmed one genuinely new external signal already missing from the repo’s research memos: `Locityper`, which is broader than the earlier `ctyper` discussion and directly targets hard polymorphic genes (`HLA`, `KIR`, `FCGR`, `MUC`, `CYP2`). I’m pairing that with the latent local stages so the

I’ve separated three buckets now: things already in the April 2 plan, things already latent in this repo but underused, and genuinely new literature-driven lanes. I’m turning that into a new memo so the output is actionable instead of another grab-bag of “possible analyses.”

I have enough signal to write now. The memo will separate “do next,” “research-only but interesting,” and “don’t waste time” so it doesn’t repeat the earlier expansion plan or smuggle in impossible work for saliva short-read data.

I’m writing a separate memo rather than touching the April 2 plan. This one is for orthogonal, higher-novelty lanes and compound analyses that fit the current sample without pretending short-read saliva can do everything.

agent_messages: Using `negative-space-sweep` with `researcher` as the search backend. First step is building the exclusion list from this repo so I don’t “discover” models or datasets you already use or already parked.

I found prior sweeps and tool scans in `docs/research/`; this isn’t a blank slate. Next I’m reading those memos to assemble the exclusion list and avoid re-suggesting already-considered models like `AlphaGenome`, `Evo2`, `ESM Cambrian`, `AbSplice2`, `VESM++`, `OpenSpliceAI`, `Splice Ninja`, and othe

The repo already has several March scans of this exact space, so I’m treating those as the exclusion list rather than starting from zero. I’m now searching for genuinely missing categories: single-cell regulatory maps, perturbation benchmarks, 3D genome models, protein function maps, and cross-popul

Most obvious March candidates are already accounted for or explicitly rejected. I’m narrowing to categories that still look underexplored in-repo: cell-type–resolved regulatory data, perturbation/MPRA truth sets, 3D genome effect models, and multimodal protein-function benchmarks.

I’ve reached the point where broad repo search is no longer adding signal. Now I’m checking a small set of genuinely underexplored candidates against primary sources: 3D-genome effect models, cell-type prediction models, newer regulatory models, and structured MAVE resources.

The promising ones so far are not “yet another generic VEP.” They’re new signal classes: `SuPreMo` for 3D genome/SV effects, `scPrediXcan` for cell-type–specific genetically predicted expression, `ClinMAVE` as a structured functional-evidence layer, and `EMO` as a regulatory model with tissue/single

I have two clear genuinely-missing candidates and a couple of “already-known but still not integrated” ones. I’m doing one last pass for promoter/MPRA-specific deep-learning models, because that’s the one model class your current stack still barely touches.
I have enough candidates. I’m pulling the key details from the source pages now so I can separate “worth wiring” from “interesting but not for this pipeline.”

Short answer: yes, but the remaining wins are narrow. I would not chase another broad “general genomics FM”; the real gap is specialized signal you still don’t have, plus better regulatory truth sets.

The genuinely missing candidates I’d put on the board are:

- `SuPreMo / SuPreMo-Akita` for 3D-gen
Using `data-acquisition` with `genomics-pipeline` and `modal`. I’m going to verify actual accessibility for the shortlist from the last sweep, not just assume the papers imply downloadable assets.

agent_messages: Using `modal`, `genomics-pipeline`, `dispatch-research`, and `project-upgrade`. First I’m checking repo state, prerequisites, recent audit artifacts, and whether the working tree is clean enough to run the full loop safely.

`--harness` changes the audit angle: I’ll focus on enforcement, typed contracts, unification, and agent-failure prevention rather than generic bug hunting. The working tree is already dirty, so I’m treating this as audit + verified plan unless I find isolated fixes that can be landed without contami

I’ve confirmed `llmx` is available and there are recent review artifacts worth reusing. Next I’m reading the session-start skills and the latest audit outputs so the harness scan doesn’t rediscover work that already exists.

The repo already has recent `project-upgrade` and `dispatch-research` history in `MAINTAIN.md`, so I’m biasing toward delta findings. Now I’m checking current harness artifacts, deferred items, and the in-flight `docs/audit/dispatch-2026-04-01` output before deciding what still needs auditing.

There’s already a fresh `dispatch-2026-04-01` bundle and the 2026-03-31 harness run captured deferred items. I’m reading those now so I can verify whether today’s delta is new work or just unfinished carryover.

The current dirty files overlap the surviving harness findings, so I’m checking whether those fixes are already in progress before I propose or touch anything. If the uncommitted diffs already cover the issue, it moves from “do now” to “verify and finish.”

The working tree already contains a real implementation pass on both surviving themes: `_STATUS.json` unification and registry-backed locus overlays. I’m validating whether those edits are coherent or if they introduced new contract breaks before I recommend finishing them.

I’m at the verification step now. The main question is whether the current uncommitted harness work is internally consistent, not whether the old findings were real. I’m probing that by running the local status/freshness entrypoints and the required genotype contract gate.

The verification gates are good so far: `verify-genotypes`, `py_compile`, and `lint-imports` all pass on the current tree. I’m waiting on `freshness --summary` now because that’s the entrypoint most likely to expose the remaining state-contract issues.

I’ve got one likely regression vector: the current `genomics_status` change broadens its scan to two status roots, but the dedupe key is the full path, so a stage present in both roots will be counted twice instead of preferring the canonical one. I’ve confirmed the repo is mid-change already, so I’m treating this as an audit, not a cleanup pass. Next I’m looking for concrete legacy seams: old Modal APIs, scripts outside the `@stage`/`Paths`/structured-status path, and compatibility code that exists only to preserve earlier execution models.
The first pass already shows a mixed codebase, not a completed migration. I’m pulling the exact compatibility seams now so the answer is about callable code and enforced process, not guesswork.

I’ve got enough structure to say this isn’t just cosmetic debt. I’m running the repo’s own migration lints now because they quantify exactly how much raw fallback and pre-convergence process is still live.
The answer is already yes. I’m pulling a few exact call sites now so I can separate “legacy architecture still in use” from “temporary compatibility that’s already fenced.”

I’m checking the process layer too, not just code. The repo still has tests and lint modes that explicitly preserve the mixed old/new world, which matters if the plan is “drop backward compatibility.”

One thing I’m verifying: the product import lint reports `CLEAN`, but there are still product files with compatibility/TODO surfaces. If the lint is undercounting, that matters before using it as a migration gate.
The product lint is undercounting. It misses helper-mediated raw loads in at least one report generator, so “lint clean” is not the same as “migration complete.”

I’ve confirmed there’s even a dedicated migration benchmark in the tree. That’s not bad by itself, but it means the current contract is still “new model must emulate old outputs,” not “old outputs are gone.”

I have enough for a recommendation, but I’m grabbing the last small bit: which product surfaces still rely on the compatibility bridge, so the refactor can be staged by consumer rather than by theory.

agent_messages: Using `modal`, `genomics-pipeline`, and `epistemics`. I’m checking the phenotype context in `~/Projects/selve` and the repo’s existing genotype-analysis surfaces, then I’ll map phenotype signals to concrete genomic follow-ups rather than generic “look deeper” advice.
I’ve loaded the guardrails. Next I’m identifying where `selve` actually stores phenotype-relevant material so I can pull concrete signals instead of speculating from repo names.

`selve` already has phenotype-genotype material, so I’m reading the highest-signal summaries first instead of raw records. Then I’ll separate what already has adequate genomic coverage from what still justifies more work.

I have the phenotype side. Now I’m checking which genomic analyses are still missing or weakly resolved, because “warrants more analysis” only matters if there’s a concrete assay or pipeline step that could answer it better than what you already have.

There are already pipeline stages for many of the plausible follow-ups. I’m pulling the ones most relevant to your phenotype and checking whether they’re already resolved, weakly resolved, or still structurally limited by short-read data.

The generic `next_actions` output is mostly actionability/variant-triage driven, not phenotype-driven. I’m switching to the phenotype-specific analysis outputs, because those are the ones that can answer your question without getting distracted by incidental VUS cleanup.

I found one possible date trap: some older genotype-phenotype docs say celiac/IgA were never tested, but a later self-report mentions they were ordered in March 2026. I’m checking that before I treat “celiac gap” as still open.

The older docs are stale on celiac. There’s a newer result: `tTG-IgA <1.0` with `IgA 190`, so that one-time celiac gap is closed. I’m filtering out already-resolved items and focusing on the phenotype signals that still have an unresolved genomic angle.

I’ve narrowed this to a small set of genuinely phenotype-linked genomic gaps. I’m doing a quick literature check on those specific gaps now so I can distinguish “interesting story” from “actually worth another assay.”
I have enough to answer. I’m pulling exact file locations for the decisive pieces: what’s already resolved, what’s contradictory, and what’s still structurally undercalled.

I’ve loaded the skill instructions and the next step is the live inventory, not ideation. I’m scanning `scripts/*.py`, stage registration, and concept keywords so the expansion work is constrained by what already exists rather than by stale docs.

The first pass inventory already shows the pipeline is broader than the static counts in `AGENTS.md`: `456` Python scripts are present, and the concept grep confirms ancestry, HLA/immune, telomere, mechanome, STR/expansion, ASE, virome, and several graph/network layers already exist. I’m tightening

The inventory gate is doing its job: several ideas that would look “novel” from a stale map are already in the tree, including `neoantigen_scaffold_inventory`, `personal_peptidome`, `haplotype_biography`, `dna_mechanome`, `most_valuable_next_data`, and multiple ancestry/HLA/telomere scripts. I’m mov

The gap scan turned up a useful pattern: some concepts are absent, but several “missing” ones are actually present upstream with weak or no downstream synthesis. I’m checking that boundary now, because wiring interpretation on top of an existing callset is usually higher-value than introducing a bra

`pgx_kinetics` killed one apparent gap. The strongest candidate so far is MEI: there is a full upstream caller (`modal_melt_mei.py`) and registry stage wiring, but I’m not seeing a downstream interpretation layer comparable to what CNV, mtDNA, PGx, or STR outputs already get. I’m verifying that agai

The local MEI artifacts changed the direction. There is no richer consumer yet, and the shipped `mei_summary.json` is not reliable enough to build on directly: its `mei_type_counts` keys are numeric scores, not MEI family labels, so I’m treating the raw TSV as the source of truth instead of extendin

I’ve found an actual negative-space candidate rather than a purely imagined one: the MEI caller is present, stage-registered, and locally summarized, but the only local artifact is a low-fidelity `mei_summary.json` with corrupted family counts and no downstream interpretation script. I’m confirming

I’ve narrowed the design space enough to justify a concrete probe. `analysis/mei` has no real consumers outside download/status plumbing, so I’m fetching the missing raw MEI TSV from Modal to confirm the file shape and decide whether the expansion should be a standalone `mei_burden` analysis or some

The MEI download is running. While that pulls, I’m drafting the shortlist and checking whether anything else competes with MEI on feasibility and novelty, so I don’t overfit to the first plausible gap.

agent_messages: Using `dispatch-research` with subagents, and I’m treating this as a full sweep unless the codebase state says otherwise. First I’m checking for any leftover background work from the aborted turns, then I’ll do recon and split the audit into 6 focused subagents with disjoint scopes.

There are several existing `codex` processes from earlier sessions, so I’m not going to stack `codex exec` on top of them. I’ll use internal subagents instead, keep them read-only, and do the verification locally so the error correction phase stays under control.

The repo already has in-flight work from today, including a half-finished `docs/audit/dispatch-2026-04-01-b/`. I’m narrowing the sweep to the current uncommitted dataset and ontology work rather than re-auditing the whole project; otherwise we’ll just rediscover old material.

I’ve got enough recon to split this cleanly. The active surface is the ontology bootstrap, bulk dataset fetchers, and the new tissue-convergence caller, so I’m dispatching subagents against those slices plus one wiring/coverage pass and one docs-vs-code drift pass.

The tool layer capped me at 6 concurrent subagents, so I’m using the sixth slot for the trait-domain pass and doing the caller-wiring sweep myself in parallel. That keeps the dispatch broad without stalling on the thread limit.

The local caller sweep already shows one likely structural split: `tissue_convergence` is actually wired into the dashboard, `open_targets_bulk` is wired via `BiomedicalClient`, but `eqtl_catalogue_lookup` and `splicevault_lookup` currently look test-only. I’m waiting on the deeper subagent passes b

I’m checking the live artifacts now, not just the code, because a few of these joins only make sense once you see the actual TSV/JSON headers. That should let me separate real contract bugs from purely hypothetical ones before the subagent reports come back.

One subagent landed a real candidate bug in the trait-domain path: ontology resolution can succeed, then still fall through to `other` because the keyword fallback uses the original raw label instead of the resolved canonical label. I’m verifying that against the live alias table and the actual runt

A second subagent found a UI-facing issue rather than substrate: the dashboard calls it a convergence card but renders `top_variants` directly, which can include rows with no shared tissues. I’m verifying the live JSON payload against the dashboard rendering logic now, because that one is easy to co

The SpliceVault finding looks real from static inspection already, but I’m reproducing it on a throwaway temp DB so I can distinguish “unsupported by design” from “silently wipes data” before I plan a fix.

## Apps
Apps are mentioned in the prompt in the format `[$app-name](app://{connector_id})`.
An app is equivalent to a set of MCP tools within the `codex_apps` MCP.
When you see an app mention, the app's MCP tools are either already provided in `codex_apps`, or do not exist because the user did not install it.
Do not additionally call list_mcp_resources for apps that are already mentioned.

agent_messages: Using `novel-expansion` with `genomics-pipeline` and `modal`. I’m starting at the mandatory inventory gate: live script inventory, concept grep, and stage registry check before proposing anything new.
The skills are loaded. Next I’m generating the live inventory artifacts the expansion workflow requires, including the concept grep file that blocks brainstorming until it exists.

There’s already a recent `CYCLE.md` trail showing several late-March expansion runs, so I’m treating this as a fresh pass that must avoid rediscovering those features. The next step is writing the inventory and concept files from current code, not from historical summaries.

The inventory gate has hard numbers now: `136` registered stages and `115` `@stage`-decorated scripts in the current tree. I’m writing the inventory artifacts next so the brainstorm phase is grounded in current code rather than filenames or stale plans.

The artifact write had a shell-expansion mistake, so the grep file contains literal command substitutions instead of results. I’m correcting that before doing any ideation; the brainstorm gate depends on the actual live matches.

The live grep shows how broad the current surface already is: ancestry, telomere, mechanome, STR/repeat, immune, and several product-layer scripts are present. I’m now pressure-testing candidate gaps by explicit absence grep rather than inventing “new” analyses that already exist under another name.

One obvious candidate was already wrong: ROH is not a gap. There’s a full `modal_roh.py`, a `roh_gnomad` stage, and downstream ROH finding assembly, so I’m tightening the absence checks instead of treating keyword hits as evidence of missing functionality.

I’m checking one more thing before external research: whether the “gap” is actually an unregistered local script with no caller. If so, the right move is wiring and validation, not inventing a second implementation.

I have one clean true gap candidate so far: branchpoint or cryptic-exon splicing. The other obvious spaces keep collapsing into existing scripts or stages, so I’m doing one more precise sweep for chromosomal mosaicism and cnLOH before I commit to a build target.
The last sweep used `noglob` in the wrong place, so `rg` tried to open the literal `scripts/*.py`. Re-running against the directory directly.

First step is preflight: check the git state, find any recent upgrade/review artifacts, and identify the shared core modules that are worth analyzing instead of dumping the whole repo

The worktree is dirty on `main`, so the skill’s normal execution path is blocked. I’m not going to start a commit-per-finding upgrade run on top of unrelated uncommitted changes; the safe path is a read-only harness scan and triage first, then execution only after the tree is clean or you explicitly

I’m pulling in the recent review artifact and mapping the shared modules now. That gives me a harness-oriented target set instead of a whole-repo dump, and it will show whether a proposed “architectural fix” is already covered by earlier work.

There was already a substantial harness run on 2026-03-31, including AST lints and several contract hardenings. I’m switching to diff-aware thinking now: compare today’s state to the last project-upgrade baseline so we don’t spend tokens rediscovering fixes that are already in `MAINTAIN.md`.

The repo has a last-upgrade baseline at `390c71f`, and `HEAD` already contains a fresh series of harness commits on 2026-04-01. I’m treating this run as a delta scan over those changes plus the current uncommitted edits, not a from-scratch architectural review.

Preflight found a hard blocker in the skill’s own rules: the worktree is dirty, with four untracked audit directories under `docs/audit/`. I’m checking whether there are recent `project-upgrade` artifacts I can reuse and whether another agent/session already owns overlapping work before deciding if

Plan for this run, given the dirty tree constraint:

1. Reuse recent artifacts first: latest `model-review` plus the 2026-04-01 harness triage, so I don’t re-scan already-known issues.
2. Run a read-only thorough scan against the current code delta, with outputs written outside the repo to avoid wor

Recent artifacts already cover today’s broader architecture ratchets, especially around `pipeline_core.py`, `wgs_config.py`, and lint surfaces. I’m still running the explicit `--thorough` scan on the current delta, but I’ll bias triage against findings that are already stale or already queued.

The repo doesn’t have the helper dump script the skill assumes, so I’m building the context bundle manually. I’m keeping it diff-aware: current file contents for the 225 code files changed since the last project-upgrade baseline, plus the main config files.

The raw changed-file bundle is too large for a useful cross-model pass: about 1.1M tokens. I’m collapsing the context to a reviewable artifact: git patch for the 69 commits since the last baseline, then I’ll selectively add a few hot files only if the patch alone is too context-thin.
The diff bundle is big enough that file assembly itself takes a moment. Once that lands, I’ll dispatch the two model passes in parallel against the patch rather than the full changed-file contents.

The source patch is still too large for a single reliable GPT pass. I’m narrowing to the highest-risk delta slice instead of feeding the model 200 mostly-safe files and getting noise back. The cut is central modules plus the largest/most-coupled edits since the last baseline.

The narrowed context is down to about 259k tokens, which is workable. I’m dispatching the two model passes now on that priority slice: Gemini for concrete bug-pattern detection in the changed code, GPT for harness and contract issues in the same delta.

The model jobs are running. In parallel I’m collecting local evidence on the same surface: targeted lint and contract tests around the changed runtime, report, and stage-target code, so the later triage isn’t model-only.

The cross-model review is running now. agent_messages: I’m mapping the gap between what this pipeline already covers and what the human genome still lets us interrogate. First I’m checking the existing stages/docs so I don’t suggest analyses you already have, then I’ll pull current literature-backed categories rather than a generic idea dump.

I’ve loaded the required pipeline skills and I’m checking the repo for current coverage so the gap analysis is real. Next step is to inventory which major genome-derived analyses already exist here versus what’s still missing or only partially explored.

The repo already covers much more than a standard WGS workflow: PRS, rare-variant triage, noncoding scoring, SVs, repeat expansions, ancestry, haplogroups, HLA, ROH/UPD, CHIP, mosaicism, pangenome SV genotyping, and several report layers. I’m now focusing on the real negative space: analyses that ar

I have the gap shape now. The remaining work isn’t “more of the same”; it splits into three buckets: things short-read WGS can still do well, things it can only do as a weak proxy, and things that need another assay or a long-read lane. I’m pulling a small set of papers for each bucket so the recomm
Semantic Scholar is rate-limited here, so I’m switching to PubMed/Scholar-style retrieval. That’s enough for this task because I need category-level grounding, not a full literature review.

I’m adding one more constraint before I write the answer: not everything biologically interesting is inferable from a singleton 30x short-read genome. I’m separating “can do now,” “can do as a proxy,” and “needs long reads / methylation / RNA / family data,” because mixing those would overstate what

You already cover most of the standard single-sample WGS surface area in [pipeline_stages.py](/Users/alien/Projects/genomics/scripts/pipeline_stages.py), so the remaining design space is not “more SNP panels.” It’s hard loci, higher-order summaries, and analyses that your current short-read lane onl

Using `researcher` and `epistemics`. I’m checking existing local plans and corpus first, then I’ll fetch full papers on the most promising missing analyses and write a dated implementation memo rather than a generic brainstorm.

I found local prior scans, but they’re still mostly abstract-level or mixed-confidence. I’m switching to a narrower deep pass on a small set of high-yield additions and fetching full texts by DOI so the plan is based on what the papers actually did, not on summaries about them.

`fetch_paper` is blocked by Semantic Scholar 403s in this environment, so I’m falling back to direct full-text retrieval from publisher/OA URLs. That still satisfies the goal here because I need the paper text itself, not the S2 metadata layer.

I’ve loaded the guardrails. Next I’m identifying where `selve` actually stores phenotype-relevant material so I can pull concrete signals instead of speculating from repo names.

`selve` already has phenotype-genotype material, so I’m reading the highest-signal summaries first instead of raw records. Then I’ll separate what already has adequate genomic coverage from what still justifies more work.

I have the phenotype side. Now I’m checking which genomic analyses are still missing or weakly resolved, because “warrants more analysis” only matters if there’s a concrete assay or pipeline step that could answer it better than what you already have.

There are already pipeline stages for many of the plausible follow-ups. I’m pulling the ones most relevant to your phenotype and checking whether they’re already resolved, weakly resolved, or still structurally limited by short-read data.

The generic `next_actions` output is mostly actionability/variant-triage driven, not phenotype-driven. I’m switching to the phenotype-specific analysis outputs, because those are the ones that can answer your question without getting distracted by incidental VUS cleanup.

I found one possible date trap: some older genotype-phenotype docs say celiac/IgA were never tested, but a later self-report mentions they were ordered in March 2026. I’m checking that before I treat “celiac gap” as still open.

The older docs are stale on celiac. There’s a newer result: `tTG-IgA <1.0` with `IgA 190`, so that one-time celiac gap is closed. I’m filtering out already-resolved items and focusing on the phenotype signals that still have an unresolved genomic angle.

I’ve narrowed this to a small set of genuinely phenotype-linked genomic gaps. I’m doing a quick literature check on those specific gaps now so I can distinguish “interesting story” from “actually worth another assay.”
I have enough to answer. I’m pulling exact file locations for the decisive pieces: what’s already resolved, what’s contradictory, and what’s still structurally undercalled.

agent_messages: Using `model-review` for a harness-style architectural review of this repo: I’m treating `--harness` as “find the highest-leverage enforcement and unification gaps,” not as a request to invent a new feature. First step is checking the repo’s constitutional anchors and local tooling, then I’ll assemb
I’m checking the review preconditions now: constitution/goals presence, current repo shape, and whether `llmx` is available before I spend tokens on context assembly.

The repo has constitution and goals, so the review can be project-anchored. I’m checking whether `--harness` maps to an existing local artifact; if it does, I’ll review that directly instead of freehanding a broader target.

I’ve confirmed there isn’t a local `--harness` artifact to review directly. I’m narrowing the target to the repo’s enforcement surface: architectural boundaries, hooks, quality gates, and the docs/code contract that AI agents depend on.

One enforcement gap already surfaced locally: the architecture data-flow task still describes `shell.nexus`, which no longer exists. I’m checking whether that’s an isolated stale script issue or part of a wider drift between code, docs, and analysis tooling.

The drift is wider than one script: `scripts/data-flow.clj`, `scripts/call-graph.clj`, and `scripts/lint-robustness.clj` still encode the deleted Nexus runtime. That’s exactly the kind of harness failure this review should pressure-test, so I’m pulling those files into context.
I have enough structural context to dispatch, but I want one more grounding pass from the repo itself: which quality gates actually run clean today, and whether the docs match the shipped commands.

`bb lint` is not clean: it fails on `src/utils/text_context.cljc` because `inc'` now collides with `clojure.core/inc'`, and there are another 67 warnings behind it. I’m pulling that into the review context because a broken advertised quality gate changes the whole harness picture.

I found a better leverage point than another stale doc line: `bb test:int` only compiles namespaces matching `^integration\\.navigation.*-test$`, but `test/integration/` contains many non-navigation integration tests. That means an advertised integration gate is narrower than the docs imply. I’m val

I have the review packet content. Next I’m writing a compact context file with only the evidence that affects the harness question, then I’ll dispatch a three-axis review (`arch`, `formal`, `mechanical`) with extraction so the output is auditable instead of hand-wavy.

agent_messages: Using `modal`, `genomics-pipeline`, and `epistemics`. I’m checking the phenotype context in `~/Projects/selve` and the repo’s existing genotype-analysis surfaces, then I’ll map phenotype signals to concrete genomic follow-ups rather than generic “look deeper” advice.
I’ve loaded the guardrails. Next I’m identifying where `selve` actually stores phenotype-relevant material so I can pull concrete signals instead of speculating from repo names.

`selve` already has phenotype-genotype material, so I’m reading the highest-signal summaries first instead of raw records. Then I’ll separate what already has adequate genomic coverage from what still justifies more work.

I have the phenotype side. Now I’m checking which genomic analyses are still missing or weakly resolved, because “warrants more analysis” only matters if there’s a concrete assay or pipeline step that could answer it better than what you already have.

There are already pipeline stages for many of the plausible follow-ups. I’m pulling the ones most relevant to your phenotype and checking whether they’re already resolved, weakly resolved, or still structurally limited by short-read data.

The generic `next_actions` output is mostly actionability/variant-triage driven, not phenotype-driven. I’m switching to the phenotype-specific analysis outputs, because those are the ones that can answer your question without getting distracted by incidental VUS cleanup.

I found one possible date trap: some older genotype-phenotype docs say celiac/IgA were never tested, but a later self-report mentions they were ordered in March 2026. I’m checking that before I treat “celiac gap” as still open.

The older docs are stale on celiac. There’s a newer result: `tTG-IgA <1.0` with `IgA 190`, so that one-time celiac gap is closed. I’m filtering out already-resolved items and focusing on the phenotype signals that still have an unresolved genomic angle.

I’ve narrowed this to a small set of genuinely phenotype-linked genomic gaps. I’m doing a quick literature check on those specific gaps now so I can distinguish “interesting story” from “actually worth another assay.”
I have enough to answer. I’m pulling exact file locations for the decisive pieces: what’s already resolved, what’s contradictory, and what’s still structurally undercalled.

agent_messages: Using `project-upgrade` because you explicitly requested it. I’m starting with preflight: working tree state, available tooling, recent artifacts, and the repo’s current maintenance context.

Preflight is clear: the tree is clean, `llmx` is installed, and there’s no obvious maintenance queue to merge with. This repo is ClojureScript/Babashka rather than the Python/JS shape the skill assumes, so I’m adapting the baseline and verification steps to the repo’s actual `bb`/`npm` workflow befo

I’ve confirmed the repo already has a better dump path than the generic skill template: `bb repomix` is native here, and the real verification surface is `bb` plus a small Playwright smoke run, not `npm test` alone. Next I’m capturing the baseline and building the analysis bundle under `.project-upg

There’s an existing `.project-upgrade/last-baseline.sha`, but this repo is still small enough for a full scan and the change set spans kernel, shell, docs, and tests. I’m taking the full-scan path rather than a diff-only approximation, because partial context on this codebase is where the model star

Baseline capture is running now: lint, kernel checks, view tests, smoke E2E, then a full `repomix` bundle. After that I’ll inspect failures first, because the model prompts need the current failure surface to avoid treating existing known breakage as “new” findings.

The baseline run is still in progress; the long pole here is the repo-native test stack rather than the LLM step. Once it finishes I’ll have an objective snapshot of what already fails before any model-generated finding gets a vote.

The baseline surfaced one real constraint and one useful signal. Constraint: the full repomix dump is slightly over the target budget, so I’m trimming the analysis bundle to code and a few high-signal specs instead of shipping the whole repo plus long docs. Signal: smoke E2E is already red before an

The raw dump was 527k tokens because it carried large docs and CSS that aren’t useful for bug triage. I’m repacking a narrower context: configs, source, tests, scripts, resources, and a few operational docs that actually constrain behavior.

There’s a same-day `model-review` artifact, so I’m pulling that in before the fresh scan. That saves re-discovery time and also helps reject repeated hallucinations if the earlier run already disproved something.

The fresh artifact already gave me one useful contradiction: an earlier `model-review` said `bb lint` was red, but the current baseline is green with warnings only. I’m treating that as a verification problem, not a fact, and keeping the prior artifact as input for triage rather than as truth.

The repo changed materially since the last full run review: recent commits touched the orchestrator, state model, local acquisition visibility, and a wide spread of Modal stage scripts. I’m letting the dashboard finish, then I’ll correlate those recent changes with where the current DAG can actually

The live state is enough to stop treating this as a clean rerun. There are three separate concerns now: stale detached apps and stale log noise, missing/downstream-only local sync for many newer analysis stages, and a DAG that has recently gained progress/frontier logic. I’m reading the planning cod

I have enough context to focus the audit. The likely leverage points are in the orchestrator itself, not the individual science code: wave construction, target resolution, stale state handling, and whether the stage registry is over-serializing around a few hubs like `deepvariant`, `vep`, `slivar`,

The first concrete issue is already clear: `pipeline_orchestrator.py plan` only schedules 99 Modal stages and leaves 55 local/manual stages out of the “entire pipeline”. If you mean a true full regeneration from CRAM through product surfaces, the current orchestrator does not do that end to end. I’m

The biggest scheduling bug is not subtle: ready stages are launched in plain alphabetical order. If `max_parallel` is below the 42-stage first wave, the orchestrator can waste slots on short side work before it starts the 5h SV lane or the 8h SBayesRC lane. I’m checking defaults and then I’ll patch

There are already uncommitted edits in the exact files I’d touch: [pipeline_orchestrator.py](/Users/alien/Projects/genomics/scripts/pipeline_orchestrator.py), [genomics_status.py](/Users/alien/Projects/genomics/scripts/genomics_status.py), and [test_pipeline_planning.py](/Users/alien/Projects/genomi

I’m checking the long stages themselves now. The orchestrator fix is worthwhile on its own, but the bigger wins may be inside `glimpse2`, SV calling, and the custom SBayesRC lane if any of those still serialize per chromosome or per trait internally.

I’m making one surgical edit: keep the existing DAG and registry intact, but change launch ordering so constrained runs start the longest downstream lanes first instead of alphabetical order. I’m adding a targeted test around that behavior rather than touching the broader stage registry.

agent_messages: Using `negative-space-sweep` plus the required `modal` and `genomics-pipeline` context. I’m treating this as a sweep of the current genomics pipeline/codebase, and I’m starting by building the exclusion list from repo docs, stage inventory, and prior research notes so I don’t “discover” work that al

I’ve loaded the sweep method and the pipeline/Modal constraints. Next I’m checking agent-infra and the repo’s own research/decision history, then I’ll collapse the existing active/parked/dropped work into a real exclusion list before I look for missing categories.

The repo already has a large “known gaps” surface. I’m pulling those into the exclusion list now so the sweep can focus on categories that are still absent, not on gap tickets and frontier tools that were already researched or deliberately parked.

I have enough of the repo’s prior work to stop treating “gap analysis” as blank space. The next pass is narrower: I’m testing candidate missing categories against the codebase and decisions so anything I surface is a true absent category, not just an unwired memo or a known deferred tool.

I’m narrowing this to structural gaps, not more tool scouting. The strongest candidates so far are around assay-validity boundaries, per-finding callability/confidence surfaces, and actual resource economics; I’m checking whether those are already implemented as first-class systems or only scattered

I’ve got three candidates that survive the first exclusion pass, but I still need one more check: whether the repo already has these as centralized systems rather than scattered scripts or caveats. After that I’ll write the sweep memo with strong candidates, conditional ones, and pertinent negatives

I’ve closed the loop on the main candidates. I’m writing a memo under `docs/research/` now; it will separate true blind spots from things the repo already knows about, so the output is useful as a prioritization artifact rather than another generic gap list.

The memo is written. I’m doing one quick pass to verify the claims in the document still line up with the repo evidence and that I didn’t accidentally smuggle in anything already covered by the exclusion list.

Wrote the sweep memo to [negative_space_pipeline_2026-04-01.md](/Users/alien/Projects/genomics/docs/research/negative_space_pipeline_2026-04-01.md).

The strongest genuine blind spots were not more scoring tools. They were: a machine-readable specimen/modality capability registry, a unified finding-
I’m checking the stage registry and review-packet structures before I lock the implementation plan. The key constraint is avoiding a second metadata system when the repo already has one.

agent_messages: Using `modal`, `genomics-pipeline`, and `epistemics`. I’m checking the phenotype context in `~/Projects/selve` and the repo’s existing genotype-analysis surfaces, then I’ll map phenotype signals to concrete genomic follow-ups rather than generic “look deeper” advice.
I’ve loaded the guardrails. Next I’m identifying where `selve` actually stores phenotype-relevant material so I can pull concrete signals instead of speculating from repo names.

`selve` already has phenotype-genotype material, so I’m reading the highest-signal summaries first instead of raw records. Then I’ll separate what already has adequate genomic coverage from what still justifies more work.

I have the phenotype side. Now I’m checking which genomic analyses are still missing or weakly resolved, because “warrants more analysis” only matters if there’s a concrete assay or pipeline step that could answer it better than what you already have.

There are already pipeline stages for many of the plausible follow-ups. I’m pulling the ones most relevant to your phenotype and checking whether they’re already resolved, weakly resolved, or still structurally limited by short-read data.

The generic `next_actions` output is mostly actionability/variant-triage driven, not phenotype-driven. I’m switching to the phenotype-specific analysis outputs, because those are the ones that can answer your question without getting distracted by incidental VUS cleanup.

I found one possible date trap: some older genotype-phenotype docs say celiac/IgA were never tested, but a later self-report mentions they were ordered in March 2026. I’m checking that before I treat “celiac gap” as still open.

The older docs are stale on celiac. There’s a newer result: `tTG-IgA <1.0` with `IgA 190`, so that one-time celiac gap is closed. I’m filtering out already-resolved items and focusing on the phenotype signals that still have an unresolved genomic angle.

I’ve narrowed this to a small set of genuinely phenotype-linked genomic gaps. I’m doing a quick literature check on those specific gaps now so I can distinguish “interesting story” from “actually worth another assay.”
I have enough to answer. I’m pulling exact file locations for the decisive pieces: what’s already resolved, what’s contradictory, and what’s still structurally undercalled.

agent_messages: Using `dispatch-research` with the session-start `modal` and `genomics-pipeline` skills. First I’m checking repo state and any leftover background work from the aborted turn, then I’ll dispatch 5+ parallel audit agents against high-leverage stability and type-safety surfaces.

There are already several live `codex` processes, so I’m not going to stack external `codex exec` on top of them. I’m switching the dispatch to internal subagents instead: same parallel audit shape, less MCP startup contention, and better output reliability.

I’ve got the routing constraint: this environment exposes OpenAI-family subagents, so I’ll use `gpt-5.4` for synthesis/verification and `gpt-5.4-mini` explorers for bounded audits. Next I’m mapping the existing lint/type/QA surface so the agents don’t duplicate obvious checks.

The repo already has a serious QA/lint stack, so the useful question is narrower: what failure modes remain agent-friendly and under-enforced. I’m splitting the sweep into seven disjoint audits so each agent stays within a small file budget and comes back with concrete ratchets instead of generic “a
Six audit agents are running with disjoint scopes. While they read, I’m auditing the lint stack locally so the final synthesis separates “already enforced” from genuinely missing ratchets.

The local lint surface is strong, but one concrete signal already stands out: `scripts/type_coverage.py` is currently failing with fresh regressions in `dict[str, Any]` returns and ad hoc JSON writers. I’m reading the ratchet itself now so I can separate “new debt to fix” from “new lint to add.”

I’m also checking the most recent audit artifacts before I endorse anything. This repo already has several dispatch rounds in `docs/audit/`; I don’t want to hand you stale or already-fixed recommendations.

One likely structural gap is already visible: typing exists as a side lane, not a hard gate. `type-check-core` and `type-coverage` are defined in `justfile`, but they are not part of `validate-code`, so regressions can accumulate while the main quality path still goes green.

The repo uses tracked wrapper hooks, not a stock `.pre-commit-config.yaml`, so I’m checking the actual hook runner now. If `type_coverage` is only documented and not actually called there, that’s a real enforcement gap rather than a style opinion.
I’m probing the existing hard gates directly now. If a current gate is already red, that changes the answer: the next stability move is to promote/enforce it, not invent a new linter.

agent_messages: Using `dispatch-research` because you asked for an autonomous audit-to-execution sweep. I’m starting with recon: project state, recent history, existing audit artifacts, and whether parallel dispatch is safe on this machine.

Recon shows no existing `docs/audit/` or `MAINTAIN.md`, and the host is already running 12 `claude|codex` processes. I’m treating that as memory pressure and switching from parallel dispatch to a small sequential Codex sweep, scoped to cross-file failure modes rather than broad speculation.

I’ve narrowed the audit surface to things this codebase historically trips over: `kernel.query` arity misuse, raw view-state mutations outside `view_state.cljs`, and Replicant keying drift. I’m checking the concrete callers first so the Codex prompts stay bounded and don’t burn turn budget on broad

The first concrete smell is in `components.lightbox`: it writes `:lightbox` at the top level of view-state, outside the canonical `:ui` subtree described in [CLAUDE.md](/Users/alien/Projects/evo/CLAUDE.md) and implemented in `view_state.cljs`. I’m dispatching Codex to check whether that’s an isolate

A second likely fault line is page restore/navigation state, not just lightbox. `restore-page` only writes `{:ui {:current-page page-id}}`; if the UI is still in journals or all-pages mode, that page switch can be a no-op visually. I’m dispatching one audit on view-state boundaries and one on page/s
I have the three audit slices. I’m checking `codex exec` invocation shape before firing them so I don’t waste a turn on a bad CLI call under current process pressure.

The first Codex audit is running. While it reads, I’m continuing local verification on the two likely regressions already visible from the repo: lightbox state shape drift and restore-page not exiting journals mode.

The page-restore issue is already confirmed locally from control flow, independent of Codex output: [editor.cljs](/Users/alien/Projects/evo/src/shell/editor.cljs#L485) renders journals first, and [pages.cljc](/Users/alien/Projects/evo/src/plugins/pages.cljc#L192) restores with only `:current-page`.

The lightbox issue looks stronger than “style drift.” `show!` adds `body.lightbox-open`, but [view_state.cljs](/Users/alien/Projects/evo/src/shell/view_state.cljs#L175) can reset the whole session atom without going through `hide!`, which leaves the body scroll-lock class behind. I’m confirming wher

The Codex CLI is noisy because it echoed its whole read trace, but the useful part is that it’s staying within the file budget and writing a report file. I’m waiting for the final message, then I’ll verify its claims against the actual lines before trusting any of it.

agent_messages: Using `dispatch-research` with the session-start `modal` and `genomics-pipeline` skills. I’m starting with recon: read the project instructions, recent history, and existing audit surface, then I’ll dispatch a small set of targeted code audits and verify every finding before proposing fixes.

Recon shows a lot of recent harness and runtime-state churn, plus an existing `dispatch-2026-04-01` audit folder. I’m narrowing the sweep to places most likely to have fresh cross-file drift from those recent commits, then I’ll dispatch only a few audits to stay inside Codex turn limits.

I’ve got two concrete unresolved surfaces already: the freshness/status split and the remaining locus shadow copies. I’m checking recent churn and the current queue so the new audits hit unresolved cross-file risk rather than re-auditing things already closed this morning.

The queue is clearer now. Today’s earlier sweep already narrowed two live items, so the new dispatch is better spent on unresolved code contracts: `freshness` persistence, the remaining panel shadow copies, and the deferred typed-JSON boundary.

I’m about to fire three Codex audits in parallel. They’re all bounded to five files each and focused on unresolved contracts, not open-ended exploration, so we get actionable reports instead of another pile of generic findings.
Codex dispatch is running. While those audits finish, I’m reading the likely target files locally so verification is immediate once the reports land instead of waiting until after extraction.
The audits are still chewing through files. I’m checking the output directory directly so I can salvage reports as soon as they materialize instead of waiting on the parent shell.

I’m seeing one likely theme already without waiting on Codex: some earlier `freshness` findings are probably stale after `13cd83b`, but the next-data path still looks under-tested and tolerant of silent fallback. I’m verifying that before I let any report survive.

I’ve got enough local context to start rejecting noise when the Codex reports finish. I’m doing two fast diffs now: whether the carrier list actually diverges from `gene_panels.json`, and whether the dementia script duplicates only membership tiers or also unique metadata we’d lose by deduping.

Those quick diffs need an actual parser, not a regex one-liner fighting zsh. I’m writing a tiny temp script under `/tmp` to compare the config and inline dictionaries cleanly, then I’ll fold that into verification.

Research axis 2: geno+pheno multimodal foundation models and 'OMNI' bio models. agent_messages: Using `modal`, `genomics-pipeline`, and `epistemics`. I’m checking the phenotype context in `~/Projects/selve` and the repo’s existing genotype-analysis surfaces, then I’ll map phenotype signals to concrete genomic follow-ups rather than generic “look deeper” advice.
I’ve loaded the guardrails. Next I’m identifying where `selve` actually stores phenotype-relevant material so I can pull concrete signals instead of speculating from repo names.

`selve` already has phenotype-genotype material, so I’m reading the highest-signal summaries first instead of raw records. Then I’ll separate what already has adequate genomic coverage from what still justifies more work.

I have the phenotype side. Now I’m checking which genomic analyses are still missing or weakly resolved, because “warrants more analysis” only matters if there’s a concrete assay or pipeline step that could answer it better than what you already have.

There are already pipeline stages for many of the plausible follow-ups. I’m pulling the ones most relevant to your phenotype and checking whether they’re already resolved, weakly resolved, or still structurally limited by short-read data.

The generic `next_actions` output is mostly actionability/variant-triage driven, not phenotype-driven. I’m switching to the phenotype-specific analysis outputs, because those are the ones that can answer your question without getting distracted by incidental VUS cleanup.

I found one possible date trap: some older genotype-phenotype docs say celiac/IgA were never tested, but a later self-report mentions they were ordered in March 2026. I’m checking that before I treat “celiac gap” as still open.

2. Audit applicability enforcement and execution planning: `pipeline_stages` / `pipeline_orchestrator` / `pipeline_cli` for mismatched
I’m dispatching three bounded code audits in parallel. While they run, I’ll verify the relevant call paths locally so I’m not waiting on them for the critical path.

I’ve got one likely contract bug candidate already: blocked stages are filtered out before dependency closure is enforced, which can let downstream dependents appear runnable if their blocked prerequisite drops out of the target set. I’m checking whether the registry actually contains stages where t

I found a second candidate that is more than a test gap: `cmd_run --resume` reloads journal targets and re-partitions them by runner, but it does not re-run applicability gating. If the active sample or config changed, a resumed run can bypass the new blocked/caution surface entirely. I’m checking `

The local verification is converging on three concrete items: one actual plan drift, one real resume-path contract hole, and one trust-class propagation miss in Finding IR. I’m pulling exact line references now and then I’ll reconcile them against the parallel audits before I write the verified plan

I’ve got enough to move from exploration to verified findings. I’m checking one last IR serializer path, because if compat exports drop applicability there’s a second IR leak beyond the ancestry adapter itself.

I’m at the plan checkpoint. No files have been edited. The next step would touch files that are already dirty in your working tree, so I’m treating the current worktree as authoritative and will patch on top of it if you approve execution.

I have enough to answer. I’m pulling exact file locations for the decisive pieces: what’s already resolved, what’s contradictory, and what’s still structurally undercalled.

agent_messages: Using `dispatch-research` with the session-start `modal` and `genomics-pipeline` skills. First I’m checking repo state and any leftover background work from the aborted turn, then I’ll dispatch 5+ parallel audit agents against high-leverage stability and type-safety surfaces.

There are already several live `codex` processes, so I’m not going to stack external `codex exec` on top of them. I’m switching the dispatch to internal subagents instead: same parallel audit shape, less MCP startup contention, and better output reliability.

I’ve got the routing constraint: this environment exposes OpenAI-family subagents, so I’ll use `gpt-5.4` for synthesis/verification and `gpt-5.4-mini` explorers for bounded audits. Next I’m mapping the existing lint/type/QA surface so the agents don’t duplicate obvious checks.

The repo already has a serious QA/lint stack, so the useful question is narrower: what failure modes remain agent-friendly and under-enforced. I’m splitting the sweep into seven disjoint audits so each agent stays within a small file budget and comes back with concrete ratchets instead of generic “a
The first parallel batch was noisy, but five agents are live. I’m adding a couple of targeted follow-ups with explicit scopes so coverage is unambiguous before I start verification.

The agents are reading. While they do that, I’m pulling the current lint implementations and contract files locally so I can verify every suggestion against the actual code instead of trusting agent summaries.

I’ve got five live agent IDs and I’m starting the verification pass as their reports come back. The goal isn’t to collect suggestions; it’s to intersect them with actual files and keep only repo-specific ratchets that would catch the next agent mistake.

I’m dropping back to direct code verification now. The repo already tells us a lot from its own lints: the most useful additions are the ones that close the remaining untyped or silent boundary patterns without exploding churn.
One remaining gap in the current gate stack is that several contracts are still ceiling-based or opt-in. I’m verifying which ones are high-yield to harden without making the repo painful to evolve.

There’s one concrete break already: `pyright` immediately complains that `scripts/pyrightconfig.json` contains an unrecognized setting. I’m checking whether that means the current type gate is partially inert or just degraded.

agent_messages: Using `project-upgrade` in `--harness` mode. I’m checking preflight state, recent upgrade artifacts, and the repo’s current structure before I decide whether this run can execute cleanly.

Preflight failed the skill’s main invariant: the repo is already dirty, with tracked and untracked changes across docs, tests, config, and `.scratch/`. I’m checking whether there’s enough context to give you a useful harness assessment without touching the tree, but I’m not going to auto-run a commi

There are no recent `code-review` or `model-review` artifacts to reuse, and there’s no `MAINTAIN.md` queue to piggyback on. I’m doing a read-only harness pass now: identify the shared core modules, sample the highest-fanout code, and look for enforcement gaps rather than point bugs.

The codebase is small enough for a harness pass without dumping 400K tokens blindly. I’m narrowing to the shared modules and the highest-fanout scripts so the findings stay structural instead of generic cleanup.

The scan is pointing at a small set of leverage points: `selve.search`, the CLI/server request boundary, and a few large connector scripts that still trade in raw `dict[str, Any]`. I’m reading those now to separate real enforcement opportunities from generic “add more types” noise.

The request boundary is looser than it should be: the server models accept raw strings for several closed sets, and the CLI redefines the same source/channel vocab in multiple places. I’m checking whether that duplication is cosmetic or whether it’s already drifting in behavior.

I’ve confirmed one big theme: a lot of the repo already knows its schemas, but it keeps them implicit in plain dicts until very late. I’m looking at the embedding pipeline now to see whether there’s a single injection point where typed entry contracts would catch mistakes across many loaders.

There’s a concrete vocabulary drift now, not just theoretical risk: the CLI advertises five channels, but the indexing pipeline also emits `reference` and `derived`. That’s exactly the kind of agent-facing mismatch harness mode is supposed to catch.
I have enough evidence for a harness triage. I’m doing one last pass on baseline health so the recommendations aren’t floating abstractions detached from the current repo state.

The repo isn’t “green by default” right now: `ruff` is already flagging 26 issues, and the test suite is still running rather than failing fast. I’m waiting for that to settle, then I’ll give you a harness disposition instead of pretending this is ready for autonomous commits.

I’m adding one more layer before I write the plan: generated-vs-source material and repo-root strays. If those are mixed into the same navigation surface, agents still waste time even after better naming.

I found a likely trap: there’s already a prior directory-restructure decision plus a simplify sweep on large-file decomposition. I’m reading those before I hand you a plan so I don’t propose something the repo already rejected or partially implemented.

agent_messages: Using `modal` and `genomics-pipeline` for repo context, then `epistemics` and `researcher` because this is a genomics/medical evidence question. I’m checking the relevant skill instructions first, then I’ll pull current primary sources on cross-species genomics, pharmacogenomics, and contraception r

I’ve got the repo/medical guardrails loaded. Next I’m checking three axes: whether animal genomes help at all for personal prediction, what human DNA already does well for drug response, and whether contraception has any validated genomics yet.

The initial pass confirms the human side has real clinical signal via CPIC/FDA. The weak spot is contraception: I’m now checking whether there are any validated gene-based prescribing rules there, because if not the answer is “don’t build that yet.”

I have enough to separate signal from hype. I’m doing one last pass on official contraception guidance and early contraception-PGx studies so the answer lands on what’s clinically usable now versus what’s still just a research bet.

Short answer: yes, but not in the way most people hope.

Animal and monkey genomics are useful for finding mechanisms and better models. They are weak for choosing a specific human’s medication or birth control. Reviews of translational medicine say animal models are good for dissecting biology but

Then don’t optimize around monkey data. Optimize around leverage.

The best non-obvious uses of personal DNA are the places where one result can change years of decisions:

1. Medication landmines.
Use WGS to build a permanent “don’t get burned later” layer: `HLA-B*57:01`, `HLA-B*15:02`, `CYP2C19`,
I’m checking the repo against those categories instead of guessing. The question is whether the pipeline already covers them end-to-end or whether some of them are only partial/research-only.

The repo already has most of the obvious pillars. I’m checking the two likely gaps now: whether family cascade is actually wired as output, and whether medication risk is dynamic polypharmacy versus just static PGx calls.
Most of the categories are present. I’m checking whether the real gap is “we have analyses” versus “we have a prioritized operating system that tells you what to do next.”

Mostly.

agent_messages: Using `dispatch-research` with the session-start `modal` and `genomics-pipeline` skills. First I’m checking repo state and any leftover background work from the aborted turn, then I’ll dispatch 5+ parallel audit agents against high-leverage stability and type-safety surfaces.

There are already several live `codex` processes, so I’m not going to stack external `codex exec` on top of them. I’m switching the dispatch to internal subagents instead: same parallel audit shape, less MCP startup contention, and better output reliability.

I’ve got the routing constraint: this environment exposes OpenAI-family subagents, so I’ll use `gpt-5.4` for synthesis/verification and `gpt-5.4-mini` explorers for bounded audits. Next I’m mapping the existing lint/type/QA surface so the agents don’t duplicate obvious checks.

The repo already has a serious QA/lint stack, so the useful question is narrower: what failure modes remain agent-friendly and under-enforced. I’m splitting the sweep into seven disjoint audits so each agent stays within a small file budget and comes back with concrete ratchets instead of generic “a

Three audit agents are running. The parallel wrapper was flaky with `spawn_agent`, so I’m finishing the dispatch sequentially to guarantee we actually hit the 5+ agent requirement instead of assuming the first batch landed.
Continuing the dispatch. While the remaining agents spin up, I’m also reading the key ratchet scripts locally so I can verify whatever they claim instead of forwarding raw subagent opinions.

Dispatch is the only messy part here. The audit itself is under control; I’m forcing the remaining agents up one-by-one instead of trusting the wrapper, then I’ll collapse everything into verified findings rather than seven unvetted reports.

I’m counting the first three internal agents as the primary sweep and adding two narrowly-scoped external `codex exec` audits to satisfy the “5+” requirement without widening scope. They’re limited to five files each and report into a dated audit folder so I can verify them like the others.

The five-agent threshold is now met: three internal explorers plus two bounded `codex exec` audits writing into `docs/audit/dispatch-2026-04-02-agent-sweep/`. While they run, I’m reading the key ratchet code paths directly so I can separate genuinely new leverage from checks the repo already has.

The two background `codex exec` jobs exited too fast to trust, so I’m rerunning those scopes synchronously and capturing the text directly. That still satisfies the extra subagent requirement, but now I actually get verifiable output instead of empty `.log` files.

The current code invalidates most of the earlier “apply” list: report assembly is already centralized, PharmCAT consumers already use the canonical extractor, and the triage stage-name mismatch is fixed. I’m checking the remaining harness debt for something that is both real and worth touching.

The remaining live leverage point is the summary-schema registry: lots of stages declare `summary_file`, but only a minority get typed validation, and there’s no explicit exemption list to stop silent drift. I’m quantifying that gap and checking whether a small enforcement hook is enough.

The tests around runtime-state/harness contracts already pass, so whatever I recommend has to add coverage that doesn’t exist yet. I’m computing the exact summary-registry gap now with the repo’s import path fixed.

I’ve narrowed it to one real harness item and one broader deferred item. Before I write corrected artifacts, I’m checking whether `.project-upgrade` is ignored so I don’t accidentally violate the clean-tree constraint of the workflow.
