I’ve loaded the research and domain guardrails. Next I’m inventorying what the repo already supports so the “new” recommendations are actually net-new, then I’ll cross-check that against recent methods and tools.

The local inventory is already useful: the repo has broad coverage, including AlphaGenome, Evo2, pangenome CNV genotyping, MEI calling, branchpoint scans, predicted expression, and several exploratory somatic/noncoding layers. The real question is no longer “what analyses exist,” it’s “which analyse

This is a `Deep` research pass. I’m splitting the search into three axes: short-read analyses we can run now, newer methods that meaningfully extend current WGS interpretation, and high-value analyses that need a new assay so we don’t confuse “new” with “actually possible from the current 30 GB.”

Semantic Scholar is 403’ing, which matches the skill’s fallback note. I’m switching the paper search to OpenAlex and direct preprint/publisher search so this stays grounded in actual sources instead of stale memory.

I’m dispatching three bounded codebase audits in parallel: somatic/mosaic, tissue/phenotype-conditioned interpretation, and next-gen PRS. The point is to separate “nice paper” from “actually a gap in this repo.”

I’m not assuming the named stages are substantive just because they exist. I’m now reading the candidate scripts directly to distinguish real computation from wrappers, proxies, and placeholders before I recommend anything.

Using `modal` and `genomics-pipeline` to keep this constrained to the PRS-related pipeline surface. I’m going to inspect only the highest-signal files from your shortlist, cap it at 6, and answer against code/docs actually present rather than infer from stage names.

I’ve narrowed it to six files: the two PRS scorers, the pathway PRS script, stage registry, the GLIMPSE2 PRS bias memo, and the recovered frontier-methods memo. Next step is reading only the relevant line ranges so the answer is anchored to the actual implementation surface, not filenames.

I’ve loaded the skill instructions and the Modal/pipeline constraints. Next I’m doing the live inventory and concept grep the skill requires, not relying on the stale map.

The repo is larger than the skill’s example assumes: `440` Python scripts under `scripts/`, with many analysis domains already present. I’m switching from broad intuition to a constrained gap search so I don’t “invent” features that already exist.

I’m turning the inventory into written artifacts now so the later phases have an explicit “already exists” baseline. After that I’ll score candidate gaps against the existing stage set and only research concepts with zero direct overlap.

The first-pass regex is too noisy because terms like `repeat` hit generic code. I’m switching to filename and docstring inventory so the “already exists” list reflects actual analyses, not incidental text matches.

The inventory confirms most obvious ideas are already built: ancestry, phasing, telomere, HLA/KIR, STRs, SVs, noncoding scoring, pQTL/PrediXcan, pathogen work, and multiple report layers. I’m checking the current cycle file now so I don’t duplicate a deferred or already-chosen expansion lane.

There’s already a fresh negative-space memo dated `2026-04-01`. I’m reading that before I pick a target, because ignoring same-day work would be how we end up re-researching the repo’s own conclusions.

The same-day frontier-asset plan is already partially realized: both `mpra_truth_import.py` and `modal_supremo_sv.py` exist. I’m checking those structural trust-surface gaps instead, because they still look absent and they have clear callers.

`finding_ir` already has a partial specimen/callability model, so that gap is half-closed. The cleaner novelty now is a stage capability registry surfaced centrally, plus maybe wiring the existing applicability model into consumer surfaces if it still dies in adapters.

The right move is not a brand-new registry file. `pipeline_stages.py` already has `StageApplicability`; the missing piece is richer machine-readable fields and central surfacing. I’m checking the existing evaluator and status surfaces before I patch that path.

Recon is narrowing the search space. Recent commits cluster around `next-data`, typed JSON boundaries, stage trust contracts, and loader/runtime unification, so I’m checking open plans and existing April audits before I dispatch any new review.

There’s already a partially completed `dispatch-2026-04-01-b` bundle in the worktree, plus a separate set of uncommitted dataset-integration files. I’m checking whether the highest-value move is to verify and finish that existing audit thread instead of launching a redundant sweep.

I’m using one sequential subagent rather than parallel fan-out because the machine is already carrying a heavy agent load. The subagent’s job is narrow: rank the existing April audit bundle by confidence and implementation value, not re-audit the whole repo.

The easy/stale items are already accounted for in `MAINTAIN.md`: freshness alignment was fixed in `13cd83b`, and next-data completeness was fixed in `c253132`. What’s left looks like either a concrete next-data dedup bug or a broader typed-boundary follow-up, so I’m reading those codepaths directly

The main live candidate is `next_actions` deduplication across next-data recommendations. I’m checking commit history and current tests before I touch it, because this repo already fixed one adjacent `next_data_contract` completeness bug earlier today and I don’t want to rework a stale report.

There’s already a regression test in the tree for the exact next-data dedup issue, and the current implementation does not satisfy it. I’m reading the rest of that test file, then I’ll run the targeted test slice to confirm this is an active breakage rather than a stale uncommitted mismatch.

The April bundle is partially stale: the two strongest next-data findings are already fixed in current `HEAD` and covered by passing tests. I’m switching the audit target to the live uncommitted dataset-integration slice, because that’s the part of the repo that still has open, unverified code movem

The current worktree has a concrete failing test in the new ontology/dataset integration slice: `classify_trait_domain("Type 2 diabetes mellitus")` returns `other` instead of honoring the configured trait override. That’s a code-grounded bug in active work, so I’m drilling into `ontology_lookup.py`

The failure isn’t in the new lookup table code; it’s in `trait_names.resolve_trait_name()`, which still does literal lowercasing instead of normalized alias resolution. I’m checking whether that helper has existing tests; if not, I’ll fix it at the helper boundary and lock it with a focused test.

agent_messages: Checking the PacBio dataset offerings against this repo's current assumptions: I’ll inspect that page, then compare it to the pipeline’s input model and any existing long-read support in the codebase.

The PacBio page is useful as a source of public HiFi benchmark and example datasets, but it is not a drop-in input source for the current pipeline. I’m checking the repo’s explicit assumptions and any prior decisions around long-read support now.

The current repo is explicit about being a Nebula/Illumina short-read pipeline, so the PacBio datasets fit as benchmarks or a future long-read sidecar, not as a straight replacement input. I’m pulling exact file references so the answer is concrete.

Short answer: not totally different, but not a drop-in for the current pipeline.

This repo is still anchored on a Nebula 30x short-read sample and a Nebula/Illumina-style CRAM/VCF workflow, not a PacBio HiFi workflow: [config/samples/markus.json:3](/Users/alien/Projects/genomics/config/samples/mark

You’re asking about two separate issues: whether Revio data matches the sequencing type this repo expects, and whether blood vs saliva matters. I’m checking the exact product/type distinction so the answer is precise.

I’ve confirmed the product distinction: Revio is PacBio’s long-read platform; the short-read PacBio platform is Onso. I’m checking whether anything in this repo is actually saliva-specific versus just metadata-specific.

The blood-vs-saliva part is mostly orthogonal. For germline work, this repo is not fundamentally saliva-dependent; if anything, a blood-derived germline sample helps some analyses and avoids some saliva caveats. I’m pulling the one place where saliva actually matters.
I have enough to answer, but I’m grabbing the exact CHIP caveat lines because blood actually helps there and I’d rather be concrete than hand-wave it.

Revio is long-read, not short-read. As of April 1, 2026, PacBio’s own materials describe Revio as a HiFi long-read platform, and Google DeepVariant treats PacBio as a separate `PACBIO` mode rather than the generic short-read `WGS` mode. Sources: [PacBio Revio](https://www.pacb.com/revio/), [DeepVari

I’m checking the official long-read toolchain before I tell you “yes.” The right answer depends on whether there’s a clean PacBio-compatible replacement for each short-read front-half stage, not just a vague belief that long reads are better.

agent_messages: Using `researcher` because this is a repo-backed research recall task. I’m checking the project’s prior notes and indexed history first so the answer reflects what’s already been researched rather than reconstructing it from memory.

The first pass found the likely canonical docs: the market landscape memo plus a dedicated batch-pricing memo. I’m pulling those now so I can give you the short/long frontier summary and the solo vs batch price bands from the actual notes rather than interpolating.

I have the main numbers. I’m checking two narrower memos now: one on short-read frontier economics (AVITI/Ultima) and one on lab batch policies, because the practical difference between “possible solo” and “only sane in batch” is usually hidden there.

The pricing memos line up. I’m doing one last pass on the “frontier” angle so I can separate current-buyable reality from the platform roadmap: Ultima, Element VITARI, SPRQ-Nx, and the absence of a real consumer long-read market.
I have enough to answer. I’m grabbing exact line references from the core memos so the summary can point you straight to the tables instead of hand-waving at filenames.
I found a same-day genomics frontier memo as well. I’m checking whether it adds anything material beyond the March pricing notes; if not, I’ll keep the answer tight and stick to the canonical tables.

One missing piece on the short-read side is the methylation frontier. I’m checking the Illumina 5-base memo because it changes the medium-term picture even though it doesn’t change today’s buying decision.

The internal conclusion from the February 26 to April 1, 2026 memos was: short-read frontier is mostly cost compression and assay consolidation, while long-read frontier is where the genuinely new biology sits. Short-read is being driven toward an institutional `$80-100` genome by Ultima and Element

I’m pulling the public pricing anchors and product pages now. The goal is to separate reagent-level claims from real service-lab prices, then map newest long-read devices against older-gen options and what each quote actually includes.

The public anchors are consistent enough now. I have three layers to compare: vendor list-price economics, public service-lab menus, and packaged services that bundle prep/QC/data delivery. I’m stitching those together so the answer shows what the headline price leaves out.

The paper is preclinical, but the post turns it into a human anti-aging story. I’m checking the risk/translation side now so the answer isn’t just “the graphs moved” but “what, exactly, is justified in humans.”

Not as written.

The paper does support a real preclinical signal. In an [Aging Cell paper published February 2, 2026](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12862434/), the authors used D-galactose-induced aging rats and mice, not humans and not naturally aged animals. They removed 6% of blood vo

No.

For a medical-longevity claim, that level of strictness is baseline, not excessive. The post crosses three evidence boundaries the paper does not justify:

- Animal model to humans
- D-galactose-induced aging to normal aging
- Correlated marrow changes to a proven causal mechanism

agent_messages: I’m treating this as a practical buy/use question, not a product-summary question. First I’m checking what’s already in your workspace on AVITI/Element, then I’ll verify current public info on AVITI24 specs, availability, and whether any real pricing is public.

I found prior AVITI research in your repo, including a March memo on service providers and a fresh April ordering memo. I’m using those as ground truth, then I’m checking official Element pages and public service menus to separate what’s real from what’s just vendor framing.

This is a `Standard` research pass. My search axes are: `official vendor claims and specs`, `actual orderability/pricing through public service labs`, and `practical human-health use cases plus limitations`.

I have enough to separate three different questions that tend to get conflated: `buying the box`, `ordering standard sequencing on it`, and `ordering Teton/Atlas multiomics on it`. The pricing picture is only clean for the first two; Teton service pricing is still mostly quote-only, so I’m using pub

I’m tightening the cost model now. There’s a meaningful difference between the old April 5, 2024 launch price, current 2026 distributor list prices, and actual service-provider quotes, so I’m treating distributor pages as `public list-price proxies`, not guaranteed transaction prices.

**Bottom Line**

AVITI24 makes sense for `human-health research`, not routine personal health care. It is a `RUO` platform for either:

- `cell-based multiomics` with Teton/Atlas: RNA + protein/phospho-protein + morphology in the same cells
- `regular sequencing` on the same box

Two important cavea

If the goal is `your own health`, the practical answer is:

- `Don’t buy AVITI24.`
- Use a `service lab` if you specifically want AVITI-based sequencing.
- Use `Teton/Atlas` only if you have a real cell-based research question, not a general wellness question.

The clean paths are:

- `Germline WGS`
